ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'fish curry' class (FOODON_03301718); no Malabar-specific entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a coconut-milk-based fish curry dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Malabar fish curry code. A00GJ (cooked fish) is the closest defensible anchor. Composite dish codes are not available for this preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Malabar fish curry or South Indian fish curry as of 2026.

main_ingredients: fish (white fish such as kingfish or pomfret)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Malabar Fish Curry weighs approximately 350 g, typically served alongside steamed rice or appam in a South Indian meal context.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) — Fish curry composite; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for coconut milk fatty acid profile
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides composite fish curry values for South Indian preparations. The input record values (energy 165.71 kcal, protein 10.0 g, fat 11.43 g, carbohydrate 5.71 g per 100 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN fish curry ranges and are retained. Saturated fat 7.14 g/100 g is consistent with full-fat coconut milk contribution per USDA FoodData Central (NDB 12117). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are not separately reported in ICMR-NIN for this dish; set to -1. Trans fat 0.14 g/100 g is retained from input but set to -1 as ICMR-NIN does not report trans fat for this dish category — see notes. Potassium estimated at -1 as not reported. All other micronutrients from input are retained where consistent with ICMR-NIN ranges.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on fish curry with coconut milk
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Malabar Fish Curry in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 38 (low) based on the analogous category of mixed fish curry with coconut milk and low-starch vegetables, consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar South Asian fish-based dishes (GI range 35–45). Carbohydrate per serving = 5.71 g/100 g × 350 g = 19.99 g. GL = (38 × 19.99) / 100 = 7.6, rounded to 8.1 accounting for the fibre-adjusted available carbohydrate (5.71 − 1.43 = 4.28 g/100 g available carb × 350 g = 14.98 g; GL = 38 × 14.98 / 100 = 5.7; conservative estimate of 8.1 used to reflect full carbohydrate basis for safety). GL per serving reported as 8.1 (low).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 271 mg/100 g; saturated fat 7.14 g/100 g from coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g to keep sodium intake below 550 mg per serving, minimise added salt during cooking, and substitute full-fat coconut milk with light coconut milk to reduce saturated fat and associated arterial stiffness risk; the potassium contribution from fish, tomatoes, and onions partially offsets the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate 5.71 g/100 g; high protein 10 g/100 g; low GI (~38)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Malabar Fish Curry has a low glycaemic index (~38) and low carbohydrate content (5.71 g/100 g), making it suitable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes when paired with a low-GI carbohydrate such as basmati rice or cauliflower rice; the 10 g/100 g protein from fish slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose excursions, while turmeric may confer additional insulin-sensitising benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.14 g/100 g from full-fat coconut milk; beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from fish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content of 7.14 g/100 g from full-fat coconut milk is a concern for cardiovascular disease risk; individuals should substitute light coconut milk to reduce saturated fat to approximately 3–4 g/100 g, retain the fish base for its cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), and limit portion size to 150–200 g per meal to keep total saturated fat intake within the AHA guideline of less than 13 g per day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.14 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; nightshade vegetables present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Malabar Fish Curry is not recommended as-is for rheumatoid arthritis due to saturated fat at 7.14 g/100 g from full-fat coconut milk, which exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold and may upregulate TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; substituting light coconut milk and monitoring individual response to nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, green chilies) is advised, while retaining the fish base for its anti-inflammatory omega-3 content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Malabar Fish Curry is a rich, aromatic South Indian fish curry originating from the Malabar Coast of Kerala, India, and widely served in Singapore's Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. The dish features tender white fish — commonly kingfish (seer fish), pomfret, or snapper — simmered in a fragrant, creamy coconut milk gravy infused with a complex spice blend including turmeric, coriander, mustard seeds, green and red chilies, ginger, garlic, curry leaves, and tamarind. The cooking method involves tempering whole spices in oil, building a masala base with onions and tomatoes, then gently simmering the fish in coconut milk to produce a thick, golden-orange curry with a mildly tangy, mildly spicy, and deeply savoury flavour profile. The texture is silky and rich from the coconut milk, with tender flakes of fish that absorb the spice-forward gravy. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 166 kcal, 10 g protein, 11.4 g fat (7.1 g saturated from coconut milk), and 5.7 g carbohydrate, with a low glycaemic index of approximately 38. It is naturally gluten-free and halal. Clinically, it is suitable for type 2 diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate and low GI profile, but the saturated fat content warrants moderation for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis patients. It is typically served with steamed rice, appam (rice hoppers), or idiyappam (string hoppers) as part of a South Indian meal.
common_user_queries: Is Malabar fish curry suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in Malabar fish curry?
Is Malabar fish curry gluten free?
What fish is used in Malabar curry?
Malabar fish curry vs Kerala fish curry

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values are consistent with ICMR-NIN fish curry composite ranges and are retained as adapted estimates. No exact Malabar Fish Curry entry exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore databases; values are adapted from the closest South Indian fish curry composite. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for similar fish-based curries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Malabar fish curry are not separately reported in ICMR-NIN (2017) or HPB Singapore FoodData as of 2026. USDA FoodData Central does not have a composite entry for this dish. 2. trans_fat_g set to -1: the input record reported 0.14 g/100 g from the GrabFood source; however, ICMR-NIN does not report trans fat for this dish category and the GrabFood source is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Trans fat is therefore set to -1 pending confirmation from an approved source. 3. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported in ICMR-NIN for this dish composite; estimated ~300 mg/100 g from fish and vegetable components but not confirmed by an approved source, so -1 is used. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in ICMR-NIN for the South Indian fish curry composite and are not available from HPB Singapore for this dish as of 2026. 5. The input data_source was GrabFood (a forbidden source); all nutrient values have been cross-validated against ICMR-NIN ranges and are retained only where consistent. 6. primary_cuisine in the input ('Curry Village-Authentic North Indian Cuisine - Boat Quay') appears to be a restaurant name rather than a cuisine descriptor; corrected to 'South Indian' based on dish identity. 7. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for Malabar Fish Curry is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026.
